structured_choices.data_source: 10,000 dermatoscopic images from dermatology clinics (2015–2020). Images sourced from a single institution or multi-site consortium with documented acquisition protocols. Metadata retained: acquisition date, lesion location, patient demographics, and annotator identity.
structured_choices.label_definition: Multi-class labels assigned by consensus of at least two board-certified dermatologists using standardized criteria (e.g., ABCDE rules, dermoscopy features). Ground truth established via biopsy confirmation where available; for non-biopsied cases, consensus of two independent expert raters. Label distribution documented; minority class (melanoma) frequency recorded.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split preserving class distribution and site distribution (if multi-site). Split percentages: 70% training (7,000 images), 15% validation (1,500 images), 15% test (1,500 images). Stratification ensures each fold contains representative melanoma prevalence. Patient-level split applied to prevent data leakage: if multiple images of the same patient exist, all images from that patient assigned to the same fold. Test set locked and untouched until final evaluation.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (normalization, augmentation parameters) fit on training set only. Image normalization (standardization to ImageNet mean/std or dataset-specific statistics) computed from training set and applied consistently to validation and test. Data augmentation (rotation, flip, brightness perturbation) applied only during training; validation and test use center crops and minimal preprocessing. Image quality filtering (resolution thresholds, blur detection) applied uniformly across all splits before splitting to avoid selection bias.
structured_choices.features: Raw pixel values (224×224 RGB images, standardized resolution). No hand-engineered features; CNN learns feature hierarchy. Metadata features (lesion location, patient age if available) optionally concatenated post-CNN to ablate their contribution. Image quality proxy (variance, sharpness metrics computed on training set) used for stratification and sensitivity analysis but not input to the model.
structured_choices.model: Convolutional neural network: ResNet-50 or EfficientNet-B3 backbone pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on the dermatoscopic training set. Architecture justified: established performance on medical imaging tasks, transferable features from natural images, manageable computational cost. Hyperparameters: learning rate, weight decay, dropout, batch size tuned on validation set. Multi-class softmax output (number of classes ≥ 3, including melanoma and benign categories).
structured_choices.baseline: Rule-based algorithm: ABCDE dermoscopy scoring system or equivalent published heuristic (e.g., 7-point checklist, Menzies method). Baseline applied identically to all three data splits using the same images. Baseline implementation standardized from published literature or provided by domain expert; threshold for melanoma classification specified a priori. Baseline receives no hyperparameter tuning (inherent to rule design), but threshold may be optimized on validation set to match operational constraints (e.g., sensitivity target). Both CNN and baseline evaluated on identical test set under identical conditions.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Macro-averaged F1-score on test set. Justification: F1 balances precision and recall, critical in melanoma diagnosis where false negatives (missed melanomas) and false positives (unnecessary referrals) both carry clinical costs. Macro-averaging weights each class equally, preventing melanoma performance from being obscured by majority benign class. Threshold for success: CNN F1-score ≥ baseline F1-score + 0.05 (5 percentage points). F1 addresses class imbalance without requiring arbitrary probability thresholds that may not transfer to deployment.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall for melanoma class): Critical for clinical deployment; no melanomas must be missed. Target: ≥95% sensitivity on test set.
Specificity (true negative rate for melanoma): Measures false positive rate; justifies clinical workflow (referral burden). Recorded and stratified by benign subclasses if available.
Per-class precision and recall: Disaggregates performance across melanoma and benign categories.
Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and area under curve (AUC) for melanoma vs. rest: Allows threshold adjustment post-hoc.
Confusion matrix: Visualizes specific misclassification patterns.
Calibration curve: Tests whether predicted probabilities reflect true melanoma prevalence in the deployment population.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (1,500 images, 15% of data) used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Grid or random search over learning rate (1e-4 to 1e-2), weight decay (1e-5 to 1e-3), dropout (0.3–0.5). Candidate models evaluated by macro-averaged F1-score on validation set. Best model (highest validation F1) selected. Validation set used only for tuning; held separate from test set. Cross-validation not necessary given dataset size (10,000 images) and dedicated validation/test splits.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: Test set is locked and untouched until final model selection is complete. A single, held-out evaluation pass conducted: CNN and baseline both applied to test set in one pass. Metrics computed; no retraining, no threshold adjustment based on test performance. Results reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap resampling of test set F1-score and sensitivity). No hyperparameter tuning on test data.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Image quality variability addressed: (1) Stratify train/val/test by image quality quartiles (computed from training set statistics) to ensure splits match quality distribution. (2) Sensitivity analysis: separately report performance on high-quality (top quartile) and low-quality (bottom quartile) subsets of test set. If CNN outperforms baseline only on high-quality images, quality is a confound; if CNN improves on both, improvement is robust. (3) Site-level stratification: if data spans multiple clinics, stratify splits and report performance per site to detect site-specific confounds. (4) Temporal validation: if temporal structure exists (2015–2018 as training, 2019–2020 as test), use temporal split to assess generalization across time. (5) Annotator agreement: record inter-rater reliability on ground truth labels; exclude low-confidence labels (disagreement > threshold) from test set or flag for sensitivity analysis. (6) Lesion location: ensure melanoma prevalence is similar across body locations in train/val/test; if not, stratify by location. (7) Patient demographics: if available, report CNN vs. baseline performance separately by age, skin tone (if recorded), sex to detect demographic bias.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Rule-Based Melanoma Classification

### 1. Data Preparation and Labeling

The experiment uses 10,000 dermatoscopic images collected from dermatology clinics between 2015 and 2020. Ground truth labels are established through consensus of at least two board-certified dermatologists using standardized dermoscopy criteria (ABCDE features or equivalent). For lesions with available biopsy confirmation, pathology findings serve as gold standard. For non-biopsied cases, consensus of two independent raters is required; disagreements are adjudicated by a third expert. The class distribution and annotator agreement metrics are documented before proceeding. Image metadata (acquisition date, lesion anatomic location, patient identifiers for grouping) are retained throughout for confound analysis.

### 2. Data Stratification and Split Strategy

A stratified random split is applied to preserve class balance and prevent data leakage. The split is stratified on class label to ensure melanoma prevalence (minority class) is represented equally in train, validation, and test sets. Critically, a patient-level split is enforced: all images from a single patient are assigned to the same fold, preventing information leakage if multiple images per patient exist. The dataset is divided as follows:

- **Training set:** 7,000 images (70%)
- **Validation set:** 1,500 images (15%)
- **Test set:** 1,500 images (15%)

The test set is sealed and locked immediately after splitting; no hyperparameter tuning, threshold adjustment, or model retraining touches this data until final evaluation.

### 3. Image Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All images are resized to 224×224 pixels using standardized interpolation (bilinear or bicubic). Pixel-level normalization (mean and standard deviation) is computed on the training set only and applied uniformly to validation and test sets to avoid data leakage. During training, data augmentation (random rotations ±10°, horizontal flips, brightness/contrast perturbations) is applied to increase generalization; validation and test images receive only center crops and minimal preprocessing (no augmentation). Image quality metrics (e.g., Laplacian variance for sharpness, histogram statistics) are computed on the training set to characterize quality distribution and are used for sensitivity analysis but not fed into the model. The model operates on raw RGB pixel values; no hand-engineered dermoscopy features are input, allowing the CNN to discover task-relevant hierarchies.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The CNN model is a ResNet-50 or EfficientNet-B3 backbone pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. This architecture is chosen for established performance on medical imaging, availability of pretrained weights to reduce training time, and computational efficiency. The output layer is a softmax classifier over ≥3 classes (melanoma, benign nevus, other skin conditions). Hyperparameters (learning rate, weight decay, dropout, batch size) are optimized via grid search on the validation set.

The baseline is a rule-based algorithm: the ABCDE dermoscopy scoring system or 7-point checklist, applied identically to all images across train, validation, and test sets. The rule-based system receives no hyperparameter tuning—the scoring rules and melanoma-decision threshold are fixed a priori from published literature or domain consensus. Both CNN and baseline are evaluated on the identical test set under identical conditions, ensuring fair comparison.

### 5. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (1,500 images) is used solely for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Candidate CNN models are ranked by macro-averaged F1-score on the validation set. The model achieving the highest validation F1 is selected. No further tuning is performed on the test set. The validation set is held completely separate from the test set; results are not reported until test evaluation is final.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Macro-averaged F1-score on the test set. This metric balances precision and recall across all classes, addressing class imbalance by giving equal weight to melanoma and benign categories. Melanoma diagnosis must avoid both false negatives (missed cases) and false positives (unnecessary referrals); F1 captures this trade-off without arbitrary probability thresholds. Success is defined as: CNN F1-score ≥ baseline F1-score + 0.05.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Sensitivity (melanoma recall):** Fraction of true melanomas correctly identified. Clinical target: ≥95% to avoid missed diagnoses.
- **Specificity:** Fraction of benign lesions correctly identified as benign.
- **Per-class precision and recall:** Disaggregates performance across all diagnostic categories.
- **ROC curve and AUC:** Allows post-hoc threshold adjustment and comparison across operating points.
- **Confusion matrix:** Reveals specific misclassification patterns (e.g., melanoma misclassified as nevus).
- **Calibration curve:** Validates that predicted probabilities reflect true melanoma prevalence.

### 7. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analysis

Image quality variability is a known confound. To address it:

1. **Quality-stratified splits:** Image quality metrics are computed on the training set; quartile bins are created. Splits are stratified to ensure train, validation, and test contain equal proportions of high- and low-quality images.

2. **Quality-stratified performance:** Test-set performance is separately reported for high-quality (top quartile) and low-quality (bottom quartile) images. If CNN outperforms the baseline only on high-quality images, quality is confounding the comparison; if CNN improves on both, the improvement is robust.

3. **Multi-site stratification:** If data originates from multiple clinics, train/val/test splits are stratified by site. Performance is reported per site to detect site-specific confounds.

4. **Temporal validation:** If temporal structure exists (e.g., 2015–2018 training, 2019–2020 test), a temporal split is used to assess generalization across time periods.

5. **Annotator agreement:** Inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) is computed on a subset of test images re-annotated by independent raters. Low-confidence labels are flagged and excluded or analyzed separately.

6. **Demographic stratification:** Performance is reported separately by age, skin tone, and anatomic location (if data available) to identify demographic disparities in accuracy.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This experiment establishes whether the CNN achieves a statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in test-set F1-score over the rule-based baseline on this specific dataset. It does **not** establish: generalization to prospective data from different clinics, populations, or imaging devices; robustness to adversarial perturbations; equivalence to expert clinician performance; or suitability for clinical deployment without further validation. Deployment would require prospective validation and regulatory approval. The experiment's scope is limited to the 10,000-image retrospective dataset with known quality and annotation processes.
